coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent probe based on boron‑nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for the rapid detection of acephate residue in vegetables and water
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mallik, Arnab
Hazra, Madhumita
Adak, Mrinal Kanti
Nag, Riya
Pandey, Akhil
Sahoo, Gobinda Prasad
coredata.dc:description: Uptake of vegetables such as potatoes, carrots, lettuce & beans containing high levels of insecticide acephate (AM) residue can pose a serious threat to the human body. In order to accomplish effective, reliable and rapid AM detection, fluorescent probe based on boron nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN c-dots) was developed hydro-thermally taking 4-amino benzoic acid and boric acid as precursors. The synthesized novel BN c-dots was characterized in detail by UV–vis, FL, DLS, XRD, TCSPC, FT-IR, AFM, TEM & XPS spectroscopy. The mechanism for the detection of AM was discussed based on static quenching. The BN c-dots exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity to AM, bringing about a linearity range of 0–80 μM with LOD = 27 nM under optimal conditions. These BN c-dots were utilized triumphantly for the detection of AM in potatoes, carrots, lettuce, beans & river and tap water with satisfactory recovery between 95 % and 100 %. This work offers precious insights and new thinking in designing a BN c-dots based cost-effective, efficient and reliable sensor in the area of food safety to curb health risks to humans.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
BN c-dots
Sensing
Acephate (AM)
Insecticide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of boron nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN c-dots)
Fluorescence QY analysis of BN c-dots
Morphological, structural and compositional descriptions of as-prepared BN c-dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The instruments facilities obtained from the economic support of Rashtriya Uchchatar Shiksha Abhiyan (RUSA) (Ref. No. 620/MC/RUSA2.0/PROJECT/19/13(11); Dated: 14.03.2019), MHRD, Department of Higher Education, Govt. of India, New Delhi and Research Center (Science), Midnapore College (Autonomous) to carry out the investigation is gratefully acknowledged. We are thankful to Prof. Ajay Misra, Dept. of Chemistry & Chemical Tech. Vidyasagar University, Midnapore, W. B. India, for allowing us to do the TCSPC measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (Scale bar is 100 nm.) of BN c-dots. (b) Histogram of size distribution of BN c-dots. (c) HRTEM image of BN c-dots displays lattice fringes d = 0.22 nm.
AFM image of BN c-dots. (a) Scale bar is 500 nm. (b) Scale bar is 200 nm. (c) Height profile and (d) Size distribution.
(a) FTIR spectra of the prepared BN c-dots. (b) PXRD spectrum of BN c-dots.
(a) XPS Survey spectrum, Deconvoluted core-level XP spectrum of (b) C 1 s (c) O 1 s (d) N 1 s and (e) B 1 s.
(a) UV–vis spectrum of BN c-dots. (b) Fluorescence emission profile of carbon dots (CDs) at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Particle size distribution in DLS. (b) Zeta potential distribution of BN c-dots.
FL spectra of BN c-dots displaying (a) photostability (b) storage stability (c) pH stability (d) ionic stability.
(a) Response in FL of BN c-dots in presence of AM. (b) Relationship between FL intensity and different concentration of AM. (c) Selectivity of BN c-dots for AM.
(a) FL lifetime of BN c-dots in presence and absence of AM. (b) UV–vis spectra of AM, BN c-dots and AM with BN c-dots.
Change in size of BN c-dots in DLS on addition of AM.
Change in Zeta Potential of BN c-dots in DLS on addition of AM.
The PL sensing of Acephate (AM) insecticide in spiked tap and river water by BN c-dots.
The PL sensing of Acephate (AM) insecticide in spiked potato, carrot, lettuce and beans by BN c-dots.
Assay performance of different sensing methods used to detect Acephate (AM) in the available reports.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent probe based on boron‑nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for the rapid detection of acephate residue in vegetables and water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Botany, Midnapore College (Autonomous), Midnapore 721101, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Uptake of vegetables such as potatoes, carrots, lettuce & beans containing high levels of insecticide acephate (AM) residue can pose a serious threat to the human body. In order to accomplish effective, reliable and rapid AM detection, fluorescent probe based on boron nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN c-dots) was developed hydro-thermally taking 4-amino benzoic acid and boric acid as precursors. The synthesized novel BN c-dots was characterized in detail by UV–vis, FL, DLS, XRD, TCSPC, FT-IR, AFM, TEM & XPS spectroscopy. The mechanism for the detection of AM was discussed based on static quenching. The BN c-dots exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity to AM, bringing about a linearity range of 0–80 μM with LOD = 27 nM under optimal conditions. These BN c-dots were utilized triumphantly for the detection of AM in potatoes, carrots, lettuce, beans & river and tap water with satisfactory recovery between 95 % and 100 %. This work offers precious insights and new thinking in designing a BN c-dots based cost-effective, efficient and reliable sensor in the area of food safety to curb health risks to humans.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of herbicides, pesticides and insecticides are galloping with rapidity day by day in order to ensure the proper supply of food demanded by today's vastly growing population. Usually, chemical substances insecticides are extensively employed to manage insects in agricultural setting owing to their low cost and high effectiveness [1]. AM, a highly malicious broad-spectrum insecticide, works by blocking the acetyl cholinesterase, increasing acetyl choline levels and enhancing cholinergic activity [2]. Mostly potatoes, carrots, lettuce & beans are exposed to this insecticide AM that does not discriminate between insects and humans [3]. Although AM is reliable and effective against a variety of insects but direct inhalation or dermal contact or intake of vegetables having excessive amount of these insecticide residue ultimately results in renal failure, neuronal damage such as sciatic nerve damage, milder leukocytosis, cancer, multiple organ failure and even death [4–7]. It is not only toxic to humans but also it has adverse effects on soil, atmosphere and water [8–10]. So, the detection of AM residue in these vegetables gets greater importance among the research fraternity for the wellness of both the public and the environment. There are numerous methods for sensitive insecticide detection namely HPLC [11], LC-MS [12], TLC [13], electrochemical analytical technique [14] and enzyme based biological sensing method [15] but the major disadvantages of these techniques are (i) highly skilled lab-technician is required, (ii) a lot of time is consumed for preparation of sample, (iii) sophisticated and expensive instruments are required and (iv) fast on-site detection is not possible. As the best-fit candidates, fluorescence-based sensors are being paid much attention by researchers to meet our target in the field of determination because of their various advantages such as rapid response, simple process, less consumption time and convenience.

In comparison to other fluorescence-based sensors [16–18], carbon dots are a far better choice owing to non-toxicity, high solubility in H2O, biological compatibility, inexpensiveness, tunable functionalities on the surface and attractive stability [19,20]. As quantum dots of non-toxic community, carbon dots have established itself as a popular topic of modern research [21]. To improve the optical properties of carbon dots, doping with hetero-atoms e.g. B (Boron), O (Oxygen), N (Nitrogen), S (Sulfur), P (Phosphorus), Cl (Chlorine) is done and reported [22,23]. Among the hetero-atoms, B and N are the most successful dopants because being neighbors of carbon in the periodic table and having almost similar size like carbon, they can enhance the quantum yield measured by fluorescence, modify the electronic structure of carbon dots further and also increase the carbon dots catalytic performance. These extraordinary features of binary doped carbon dots inspire researchers to construct fluorescence sensors based on BN c-dots for easy and fast on-site detection of pollutant AM in vegetables such as potatoes, carrots, lettuce and beans.

The novelty in this work is (i) we utilize new precursors 4-amino benzoic acid and boric acid for the synthesis of high quantum yield BN c-dots with the aid of hydrothermal method and build up BN c-dots based efficient and authentic fluorescence sensor for detection of AM, a potential pollutant selectively. Static quenching in fluorescence of BN c-dots is observed on binding with the insecticide. (ii) Our newly constructed sensor exhibits good selectivity to AM with low limit of detection (27 nM). The practicability of sensor was tested taking real samples such as potatoes, carrots, lettuce, beans & river and tap water with excellent recoveries of 95–100 %. During our investigation, we took the help of UV–vis, FL, TCSPC, DLS, AFM, XRD, FTIR, TEM & XPS. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports of sensing of carcinogenic AM by BN c-dots.

4-amino benzoic acid, boric acid, acemain (contains 75 % acephate as an active ingredient), dinoseb, captan, dithianon, lindane, sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), aluminium chloride (AlCl3), calcium chloride (CaCl2), tetrabutyl ammonium salts of fluoride, bromide, chloride and iodide, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4.12H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4.2H2O), quinine sulphate, whatman filter paper and other required chemical reagents were bought from the S.D. Fine-Chem Limited. Vegetables such as potatoes, carrots, lettuce & beans were collected from the local market in Rajabazar, Midnapore. Throughout the entire investigation, we use Milli-Q water. Thoroughly washing of all glassware was done by using aqua regia and then by Milli-Q H2O.

We recorded UV–vis absorbance and emission with the help of a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Labard Technologies) and an F-7000 fluorometer (Hitachi) respectively. X-ray diffractometer (Bruker) using CuKa radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm) on dry samples in the 2θ range of 5–80° was applied for the PXRD study. For investigation of the average size of c-dots, AFM (Park System, South Korea) and JEOL-JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study were done. To prepare the sample in TEM study a drop sample was placed onto a carbon supported film through a copper grid followed by the evaporation of solvent in a vacuum. Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZEN3600 was used for measuring distribution of size and zeta potential of the nanoparticles. For further characterization, FTIR spectrophotometer Perkin Elmer (Spectrum RX1), XPS (JPS-9030, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), TCSPC spectrometer [a picosecond diode laser (IBH, UK), under ambient conditions with the detection wavelength at 450 nm for the CDs solution and the wavelength of excitation was 288 nm for the samples] were operated. Origin software was used for the visualization of all the data. Milli-Q water plant (Merk Millipore), lyophilizer (LaboGene), centrifuge (Hitachi) and autoclave (India Instruments) were used in the entire experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of boron nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN c-dots)
ce:section.ce:para: By a facile hydrothermal treatment, BN c-dots were synthesized. Firstly, 0.35 g of 4-amino benzoic acid and 0.25 g of boric acid were taken in a 100 mL beaker, 25 mL Milli-Q water was added to it. After sonication for about 15 mins, we got a clear solution which was transferred into a Teflon-lining autoclave and was positioned in a hot oven at 180 °C for 24 h. After heat treatment, the autoclave was kept for cooling and finally, a transparent solution was obtained. The solution was then exposed to centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 5 min and then filtration by Whatman filter paper for elimination of large particles. Finally, the product was lyophilized for preservation in powder form for further detailed characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence QY analysis of BN c-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
With the help of relative method, QY of BN c-dots is obtained using the following equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
Q a
%
=
Q st
.
m a
/
m st
.
n a
2
/
n st
2
Where Q signifies QY, n refers to the refractive index and m represents the slope value. The subscript “st” and “a” indicate standard reference and the unknown BN c-dots. [here, the standard reference is quinine sulphate (in 0.1 M H2SO4) (QY = 54 %)].

The Method of PL sensing of Acephate (AM) is given here. Firstly, the powder form of BN c-dots was dissolved in Milli-Q water to make a concentration 0.2 mg/mL. The PL intensity of blank BN c-dots was documented as F0. After that, AM solution was added to BN c-dots, waited for 15 mins and the intensity of the resulting solution was checked by PL spectrophotometer. The intensity of BN c-dots was quenched in addition to the AM solution denoted as F. We examined the quenching in addition to the AM solution for different concentrations. The results showed that up to 200 μM concentration of AM, the quenching of BN c-dots persisted. Then phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of different pH were made using 0.1 mol/L sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4.12H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4.2H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) solutions to study the pH effect on AM content assay result. BN c-dots solutions of varied pH were prepared by mixing PBS buffer of different pH with concentrated solutions of BN c-dots.

In order to examine the selectivity of the probe, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I− along with four insecticides namely dinoseb, captan, dithianon and lindane were used. We repeated all the studies twice for consistent results. Selectivity of BN c-dots for AM was observed clearly.

In order to check the ability of sensing of the prepared BN c-dots, we performed recovery test using water collected from local river, Midnapore, West Bengal. Prior to start the test, river water was filtered by using 0.22 micro-molar membrane and then centrifuged for 20 min at 9000 rpm. Preparation of AM solution was done using river water and tap water, where, BN c-dots (0.2 mg/mL) were added to these water samples. AM solutions of concentration 0.03–0.2 mg/mL were prepared. Different concentration of AM was spiked with BN c-dots. Final analysis on the spiked tap water and river water samples were done using our sensing procedure and also recovery efficiency was measured using the following equation (Eq. (2)).
(2)
% Recovery =
Detected
AM
in
mg / L
Added
AM
in
mg / L
× 100

Similarly, 5 g of potatoes, carrots, lettuce and beans sample with 10 mL water were taken and mixed thoroughly for spiking with proper concentration of AM. Here recovery efficiency was also calculated using the procedure same as local river water and tap water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural and compositional descriptions of as-prepared BN c-dots
ce:section.ce:para: For the identification of the morphology and structure of the synthesized BN c-dots, AFM & TEM were executed. TEM picture in Fig. 1a indicates that BN c-dots have spherical shape having size ~3–4 nm without any apparent aggregation. Average particle diameter estimated from the histogram in Fig. 1b is 3.5 nm. Fig. 1c shows High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of BN c-dots displaying lattice fringes d = 0.22 nm.

Fig. 2a and Fig. 2b displays the 2D AFM image of BN c-dots and the height profile and particle size distribution is given in Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d respectively. The AFM data clearly supports the TEM data. From the AFM results, it is proved that BN c-dots are well-dispersed with spherical shape.

FT-IR analysis was performed to investigate the type of functional groups and their linkages on the surface of BN c-dots. The peaks at 3461, 3228 and 2979 cm−1 are indicative of OH, NH and CH stretching vibration respectively [24,25]. This spectrum (Fig. 3a) showed peaks also at 1657, 1591, 1284, 1123, 1068 and 1411 cm−1 which are respectively associated with CO, CN, B-OH, BC, B-O-C and BN stretching vibration [26–31]. There are several peaks in the 610–900 cm−1 region and N-B-O bonding seems to be present from the peaks at 932 and 964 cm−1 [28]. From the FT-IR data, the presence of amino, hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic acid groups and the doping of B and N on the surface of CDs is confirmed. We can expect about the super dispersibility of BN c-dots in H2O owing to the existence of such polar groups.

The phase, structure and the amount of crystallinity of the BN c-dots are examined by PXRD study (Fig. 3b). The PXRD pattern displays four peaks at ~36°, 34°, 30° and 21.8° for lattice spacing (graphite) C (100), (110), (101) and (002) respectively denoting unsystematic arrangement of carbon atom on the amorphous characteristics oxygen group containing surface [32]. Genc et al. reported such type of polycrystalline arrangement for blue, green, and red types of c-dots in the crystal structure [33]. Generally, the graphitic carbon peak in the C (002) plane appears at 26° (2θ). Here in our study the peak for the C (002) plane occurs at 2θ = 21.8° indicating the nature of the material as moderately amorphous due to surface functionalization by the dopant's nitrogen and boron in carbon dots. Again, the d-spacing (interplanar distance) value for C (002) plane of BN c-dots calculated using Bragg's eq. 2d = nƛ/sinθ (where the terms have their usual meaning.) is approximately 0.24 nm which is well matched with the d- spacing value (0.22 nm) obtained from lattice fringes of HRTEM. The results clearly indicate the crystalline nature of BN c-dots. Our PXRD data is well consistent with the recently published carbon dot materials [32,34].

For further penetration into the composition of BN c-dots, we take the help of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Characteristic peaks for C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and B 1 s is revealed in the survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 4a) and the atomic % C = 48 % N = 9 % B = 13 % and O = 30 % is obtained from spectra. The appearance of four peaks in high-resolution C 1 s in Fig. 4b spectrum at 289.1, 286.3, 285.3, and 284.6 eV correspond to CO, CC, CN and CB respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum's peak in Fig. 4d at 400.4 eV is due to BN. The high resolved spectrum of O 1 s in Fig. 4c is deconvoluted into 2 peaks first at 533.3 eV for CO and the latter at 524.1 eV for OH. Two components are seen in B 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4e at 191.3 eV and 193.2 eV which are due to BN and BO bonds respectively. Our XPS data [35–40] is well comparable with the newly published hetero-atom doped carbon dot materials which clearly prove that the boron nitrogen co-doped carbon dots are prepared successfully.

The absorption and FL emission spectra are taken to examine the optical properties of our BN c-dots. The absorption spectrum in Fig. 5a consists of two peaks first at 274 nm for π-π* transition [38,42] of the BN c-dots aromatic core and second at 247 nm owing to n-π* transition [41] of the CO and CN functional groups over the BN c-dots surface. The peak of emission is at 341 nm in the FL spectra in Fig. 5b. We obtain the highest emission intensity on excitation at 305 nm (emission wavelength starts at 315 nm and ends at 550 nm, excitation and emission slits are 5 mm each) and excitation in-dependency in emission is seen (shown in Fig. 5b). Emission Centre remains unchanged even when excitation wavelength changes. The mean PL lifetime found for our synthesized BN c-dots is 7.71 × 10−10 s. The PL QY of BN c-dots obtained is 82 % taking quinine sulphate (in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference.

DLS analysis is done for the information regarding the size and apparent zeta potential of BN c-dots. The average size of BN c-dots obtained from the DLS result is higher slightly than the TEM value because of the hydrodynamic radius of BN c-dots. The charge in the surface of BN c-dots is analyzed by Zeta-sizer in the form of zeta potential in DLS. The zeta value of −20.2 mV clearly guarantees that the surface of colloid-ally stable BN c-dots is filled with the moieties that are negatively charged. The size distribution and zeta potential in DLS is shown in Fig. 6a & Fig. 6b.

To check the PL stability of BN c-dots, thorough investigation under various conditions is done. Upon irradiation of UV light for 75 min, BN c-dots maintained stable emission, establishing its super photostability (Fig. 7a). It is observed in Fig. 7b that PL intensity of BN c-dots remained stable and steady even after one month. Also, the stability of BN c-dots is verified in PL in Fig. 7d using high concentrations of NaCl (up to 1 M) and different pH condition. No considerable alteration in PL intensity is seen under different concentrations of NaCl [43]. The intensity changes with pH and the maximum intensity is found at neutral and acidic pH (Fig. 7c). These studies ensure the regarding the excellent stability of BN c-dots in PL.

For achieving the highest performance of the probe, the experimental conditions were optimized. The pH effect on the PL intensity of the sensor was checked and the pH sensitivity of the process is revealed. For AM insecticides, quenching efficiency in PL enhanced on enhancing pH, highest at pH =7 and after that decreases. So, we choose pH =7 as the optimized pH condition for the experiment. An increment in concentration of AM leads to lowering in PL intensity of the sensor. Investigation regarding the specificity of BN c-dots for AM is done in presence of metal ions such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I− along with dinoseb, captan, dithianon and lindane (Fig. 8c). Selectively detection of AM is possible in other insecticides presence.

Allured by the excellent fluorescence property of BN c-dots, we examine it's analytes detection ability based on PL turn-off behaviour. A good sensitive detection of AM is exhibited by our BN c-dots following PL turn-off. Quenching in PL emission of the BN c-dots is seen in presence of AM (Fig. 8a). With enhancing the concentration of AM (in the range 0–200 μM), turn-off response in PL is exhibited by BN c-dots and plotting of F0/F vs [AM] shows linearity within 0–80 μM range (Fig. 8b). We also investigate the degree and the nature of quenching. Generally, quenching process in PL consists of (i) static quenching (ii) dynamic quenching (iii) IFE (iv) FRET & (v) PET. In case of static quenching, there is a stable non-fluorescent complex formation by the surface of BN c-dots with the quencher. In dynamic quenching there is a collision between excited BN c-dots and the quencher and the process involves transfer of energy and electrons. The connection between the quencher and BN c-dots is essential for both types of quenching. FL lifetime of BN c-dots does not change in case of static quenching but alteration in FL lifetime of BN c-dots is observed in case of dynamic quenching. In FRET and IFE, the emission or excitation spectra of BN c-dots is overlapped with the quencher's absorption spectra [44,45] but in the case of PET, there involves electron transfer between BN c-dots and quencher and PL lifetime is changed [46].

In order to establish the probable fluorescence sensing mechanism, we check the nature of quenching using the Stern-Volmer (StVm) mathematical statement, then by TCSPC measurement and also observing the change in size and zeta potential in DLS.
(3)
S t
V m
Equation :
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K StVm
AM
Here, F0 and F are PL emission intensities of bare BN c-dots and in insecticide's presence respectively. KStVm is StVm const. [AM] is insecticide concentration.

In this case, we also measure the limit of detection (LOD) by the equation.
(4)
LOD = 3.3
σ / Slope
Where σ = standard deviation in response in PL, Slope is calculated from the plot of PL intensity (at the maxima) vs Conc. Of AM.

In order to test the applicability and reliability of our planned sensor, we perform analysis in the detection of AM residue in vegetables such as potatoes, carrots, lettuce & beans and also in tap and river water by a spiking experiment. Accuracy is examined by taking practical samples of water and vegetables using the method of standard addition. The tap, river water and also vegetables such as potatoes, carrots, lettuce & beans samples undergo spiking with various concentrations of AM. As depicted in Table 1 & Table 2 , the satisfactory recovery of 95–100 % is seen ensuring the practicality and reproducibility of the planned sensor. So, it can be successfully implemented in real practice to detect AM. A comparison of different methods for Acephate determination is displayed in Table 3 .

In summary, taking new precursors 4-amino benzoic acid and boric acid, we successfully synthesized high quantum yield carbon dots with the help of hydrothermal treatment and employed it for effective, rapid on-site detection of AM. The synthesized green fluorescent BN c-dots were characterized optically and structurally using different analytical techniques. Here the fluorescence sensing of AM followed static quenching mechanism with LOD = 27 nM and sustained a linearity response from 0 to 80 μM. The practicability of BN c-dots was examined by taking real samples such as potatoes, carrots, lettuce, beans & river and tap water with excellent recoveries of 95–100 %. So, it can be concluded that this efficient and authentic probe with low toxicity, greater bio-compatibility is apt for the detection of AM residue in potatoes, carrots, lettuce, beans & river and tap water. Our approach in this study would have greater application potential in the realm of food safety.

Arnab Mallik: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Madhumita Hazra: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Mrinal Kanti Adak: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation. Riya Nag: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Akhil Pandey: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Gobinda Prasad Sahoo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

A. M. and G. P. S. designed research; A. M., M.H., M.K.A., R.N., A. P. and G. P. S. performed research; A. M., M.K.A., and G. P. S. analyzed data; and A. M., M.K.A., M. H. and G. P. S. wrote the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Gobinda Prasad Sahoo reports was provided by Midnapore College (Autonomous). If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

No. 620/MC/RUSA2.0/PROJECT/19/13(11); Dated: 14.03.2019), MHRD, Department of Higher Education, Govt. of India, New Delhi and Research Center (Science), Midnapore College (Autonomous) to carry out the investigation is gratefully acknowledged. We are thankful to Prof. Ajay Misra, Dept. of Chemistry & Chemical Tech., Vidyasagar University, Midnapore, W. B., India, for allowing us to do the TCSPC measurement.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Present status of insecticide impacts and eco-friendly approaches for remediation-a review
Environ. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Barathi, N. Sabapathi, S. Kandasamy, J. Lee, Present status of insecticide impacts and eco-friendly approaches for remediation-a review, Environ. Res. 240 (2024) 117432.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation of acephate and its intermediate methamidophos: mechanisms and biochemical pathways
Front. Microbiol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Lin, S. Pang, W. Zhang, S. Mishra, P. Bhatt, S. Chen, Degradation of acephate and its intermediate methamidophos: mechanisms and biochemical pathways, Front. Microbiol. 11 (2020) 2045.
